actions.thought: 5s 6h is a weak offsuit hand. Although I am on the button in a three-handed game, this hand is generally not strong enough to open with, especially at the start of the tournament when I have no reads on the opponents. I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

Being in the small blind with Td 4d heads-up against the big blind, completing the blind is a reasonable way to see a flop. The hand has some post-flop potential and playing it is standard in a heads-up situation where the button has already folded.

With 8d 7d in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind complete, checking is a solid play. 87s is a great speculative hand that plays well post-flop in position. Raising is an option to attack the weak limp, but checking keeps the pot small, guarantees we see a flop, and lets us leverage our position post-flop without risking more chips pre-flop.

The flop of Jh-9c-Kd hits the board quite hard for the Big Blind's range. My Ten-Four hand is extremely weak, holding only a gutshot straight draw with no pair. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a board that is difficult to represent. Checking is the correct fundamental play to preserve my stack and potentially take a free card on the turn.

On a highly coordinated board like K-J-9, my 8-high with weak backdoor draws has very little equity. The Small Blind's limping range often connects with this board (broadway cards, suited connectors, Kx, Jx, 9x). Betting as a bluff is unlikely to succeed at a high enough frequency to be profitable. Checking behind is the best play to maintain pot control and see a free turn.

I have 10-high with no showdown value and no meaningful draw on a board of Jh-9c-Kd-3s. Since both players checked the flop, the pot remains small. Betting now would be a bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from a player who checked behind on the flop, as they could easily have a pair or a piece of the board. Checking is the most prudent strategy to keep the pot controlled and potentially see a free river card.

The 3 on the turn doesn't change the texture of the board. Flashlight has checked twice, which often indicates weakness or a marginal made hand, but with 8-high and no draws, I have virtually zero equity if called. Attempting to bluff on a K-J-9 board is risky because opponents can easily have a piece of the board or a draw that they won't fold to a single bet. Checking to take a free river and evaluating from there is the safest and most standard play.

I have Ten-high with no showdown value on a board of Jh-9c-Kd-3s-5h. My hand has completely missed the board, and there is no pair to represent. Since the pot is small and the action has been checked through by all players, there is no realistic chance of winning with a bluff against a checking range. Checking is the most prudent decision to conclude the hand without risking additional chips.

With 8-high on the river, I have absolutely zero showdown value and will lose to almost any hand, including missed draws with A-high or Q-high. Flashlight has shown extreme weakness by checking down all three streets on a K-J-9 board, indicating they likely have a busted draw or a weak high card that they are ready to give up. Betting is the only way to win this pot. A bet of 150 into 200 is a very profitable bluff here, as it puts enough pressure on marginal hands and high-card holdings to force a fold.

I checked the river with Ten-high, and my opponent has now bet 150 into a 350 pot. With absolutely no showdown value and a board that missed my range entirely, calling would be purely speculative and mathematically unsound. Even though the price is decent, my hand is extremely weak and likely loses to any pair or even a better high-card hand that checked the turn for pot control. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

With Ace-Eight offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a strong positional advantage. This is a clear opportunity to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that balances building the pot with putting pressure on the opponents in the blinds.

Holding 8-4 offsuit in the small blind facing a 3bb raise from the button. This is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability both pre-flop and post-flop, especially out of position. The only sensible action here is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K7o. The Button (Flashlight) raised to 3x (300). The Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. K7o is a standard defense hand in the Big Blind against a Button open, especially with 100BB stacks. I have a decent King-high and reasonable odds to see a flop, although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I missed this flop entirely with my K7o. The board is quite wet with multiple straight and flush possibilities that likely connect well with a Button opening range. Being out of position, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor and most likely fold to any significant bet given the lack of equity.

I have middle pair on a coordinated board (Q-8-9 with a flush draw) and I am in position. Given that I raised pre-flop, a check behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity, as betting might force out hands I beat while being called by hands that have me dominated or have significant equity against me.

The flop checked around on a very wet board (Qs-8d-9d), which often indicates that Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) has a marginal hand or missed entirely. With King-high (Kc-7h), I have some very thin showdown value but not enough to justify a value bet, and a bluff here might be ineffective against a range that includes many middle pairs or draws that checked back. I will check to see a free river and re-evaluate based on the final card and my opponent's action.

I hold a pair of 8s with the Ace of Diamonds, which provides me with both middle pair and the nut flush draw on this board. My opponent has checked twice, suggesting they do not have a strong Queen or better and are likely playing a marginal range. By betting here, I can take the initiative, fold out equity from their weak hands, and build a pot in case I hit my flush on the river, rather than checking back and giving a free card to an opponent who might be drawing.

I have King-high (Kc 7h) with no draws on a coordinated board of Qs-8d-9d-4s. After the flop checked through, Flashlight bet 350 into a pot of 650 on the turn. Since I have no showdown value and no equity to improve to a winning hand, folding is the only logical choice.
I am on the Button with K4o in a 3-handed game. While button opening ranges are wide in 3-max, K4o is generally too weak to open and is a standard fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The button (Pro) has already folded. While it only costs 50 more to complete the bet, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind (Flashlight) is not profitable in the long run. I will take the disciplined route and fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this hand is at the very bottom of a potential opening range. Given the early stage of the tournament and the weak post-flop playability of T7o, I will fold and preserve my stack for more profitable opportunities.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Five suited. With the button folding, I am heads-up against the big blind. Calling the additional 50 to see a flop is a reasonable strategy here, as it allows me to see the board cheaply with a hand that has some playability and backdoor flush potential.
With 7s3c offsuit in the Big Blind after the Small Blind limps, I have a very weak hand. Checking is the only sensible play to see a free flop.

The flop of 9-6-2 with two diamonds is excellent for my hand, as I hold the Queen of diamonds, giving me a flush draw. With the opponent checking, I want to take the initiative and semi-bluff to either win the pot now or build equity. Betting 100 into the 200 pot represents strength and puts pressure on the opponent's range, potentially taking the pot down immediately or setting up a multi-street bluff.

With 7s3c on a 9s 6d 2d flop, I have completely missed the board, holding just 7-high with no viable draws. Flashlight has initiated the action by betting 100 into a 200 pot. With zero equity and a weak hand, folding is the only profitable long-term decision.

I hold 4c 7d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited hand with almost no potential to make strong pairs or draws. Playing this hand from the button offers no strategic advantage and carries high risk relative to reward. Preserving my stack for more playable holdings is the correct mathematical decision in the early stages of a tournament.

48 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind facing the Big Blind. Trying to steal with such a low equity hand out of position is unprofitable. Folding is the correct decision.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game and am first to act pre-flop. I hold 8-2 offsuit, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no positional advantage post-flop against the blinds and a completely unplayable hand, the clear and optimal decision is to fold.

K9o is a relatively strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation after the button has folded. While I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, K9o is well ahead of the random range the Big Blind (Flashlight) holds. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to either take the pot down immediately or build value and maintain initiative.

Flash raised to 300 from the small blind. I am in the big blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Given the pot odds, where I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 600, calling is mathematically justified despite the weak hand. I have position on the small blind post-flop, allowing me to potentially exploit a check or take control if the flop misses the small blind's raising range.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have the initiative. The flop of 3c-Tc-5s is relatively disconnected, although the Ten is a card the Big Blind could have hit. However, my range as the small-blind raiser includes many strong hands like overpairs or a strong Ten. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to apply pressure on the opponent's defending range and potentially take the pot down now or set up a turn barrel.

The board is 3c-Tc-5s, and I am holding Qs-2c. I have no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak backdoor flush draw. My opponent (Flash) bet 300 into a 600 pot, indicating potential strength or a standard continuation bet. With very little equity to improve to the best hand, calling is mathematically unjustified and likely to result in further losses on later streets. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Queen-Four offsuit is a weak hand even in a three-handed game. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, this hand ranks poorly in an opening range. Given the early stage of the tournament, I will play disciplined and fold this hand rather than risking chips with a sub-optimal holding.

Ten-three offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind against the big blind. With 100 big blinds deep, there is no reason to invest additional capital into a marginal situation. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for higher equity opportunities.

My hand is 2d, Tc, which is extremely weak and has very little equity post-flop. Playing this hand from the button does not justify calling the 100 chip bet to see the flop, as it will be difficult to make a strong hand. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities in this 150-hand tournament.

My hand is 74o, which is statistically very weak and mostly unplayable. Being in the small blind means I will be out of position post-flop if I limp or raise and get called. The standard play here is to fold and preserve chips for better starting hands.
In 3-handed play, Q7 suited is a reasonable hand to open from the Button to steal the blinds or play in position post-flop. I'll make a min-raise to 200.

Ace-Trey offsuit is a relatively weak hand to play out of position, especially against a button open from Pro. While the call is only 150 more, playing out of position for the rest of the hand with a weak Ace often leads to difficult post-flop scenarios where I am dominated or forced to fold to aggression. A 3-bet bluff is an option, but at this stage of the tournament, folding is the more disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack.

Defending the big blind with Ace-Trey offsuit against a button min-raise is a standard play. The pot odds are favorable (100 to call to win a pot of 350), and Ace-high is strong enough to see a flop and realize equity.

With Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw on a flop of 8c-5s-Ts, I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. Leading into the opponent offers no strategic advantage. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and see if the opponent decides to continuation bet, allowing me to evaluate my options.

My hand, Queen-Seven suited, completely missed the 8-5-10 flop. This wet and coordinated board hits the Big Blind defender's range quite well, as they can have many pairs, straight draws, and flush draws. Since I have almost no equity, a continuation bet is likely to get called or raised. Checking back is the best option to take a free card, control the pot, and evaluate the turn.

I hold Ace-high with no significant draws on a board of 8c-5s-Ts-7c. My opponent checked back the flop, which often indicates marginal value or a decision to take a free card. Since I have no equity to speak of and am out of position, betting at this pot as a bluff is high-variance and likely unnecessary. Checking remains the optimal strategy to control the pot size, see if my opponent checks behind again, or re-evaluate on the river.

I turned a pair of sevens, giving me some marginal showdown value, but the board is very wet with two flush draws and straight possibilities (8-5-10-7). Flashlight checked twice, which could indicate weakness or a draw, but betting my weak pair here exposes me to check-raises and mostly gets called by better hands or strong draws. Checking behind for pot control and taking a free river card is the most prudent play.

I am at the river with Ace-high (Ad-3s). The board is 8c-5s-Ts-7c-4c. My opponent checked the flop and the turn. The board is quite connected, offering straights (6-9) and a potential flush with the three clubs on board. With Ace-high, I have very little showdown value, but betting here is a pure bluff into a pot that has been checked down twice. Betting would likely force out hands I beat (like K-high or Q-high) and get called only by hands that beat me. Checking is the most standard and disciplined play to get to showdown or surrender to a bet if the opponent decides to value-bet their marginal hands.

The river brings a third club and completes some straight draws, making the board 8-5-10-7-4. I have third pair with a Queen kicker. Flashlight has checked all three streets, indicating likely weakness, a missed draw (like spades or a straight draw), or a weak pair. However, a bet here has no value since worse hands will almost certainly fold and better hands (any 8, 10, or flush/straight) will call or even raise. Checking behind allows me to realize my marginal showdown value for free and potentially win against missed draws.

I am on the Button with Pocket Jacks (Jd Jc), which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to build the pot and take control of the hand. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard and effective play from this position to narrow the field or win the blinds immediately.

Holding K8o in the small blind against a button open is a marginal situation, and being out of position makes it even less desirable to play. Calling creates a difficult post-flop scenario where I will be playing check-fold or check-call out of position, and 3-betting is too aggressive for this specific holding. Given the stack depths and the fact that the big blind is yet to act, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to conserve stack size.
Holding 7c 2s (seven-deuce offsuit), the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x open raise from the Button, I will instantly fold.

6s-Th is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, making it difficult to play profitably against two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and conservative choice to maintain stack integrity in this 3-handed game.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind facing the Big Blind with Jack-Eight suited. This is a solid hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. By raising to 300, I take the initiative, giving myself a chance to win the pot pre-flop while also building a pot with a hand that plays reasonably well post-flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit (T3o). The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a 3x open. T3o is a very weak hand with poor equity and post-flop playability. Defending the big blind with this hand against a raise from the small blind is generally a losing play over the long term. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K7o on the button in a 3-handed game is a marginal holding. While it can sometimes be opened as a steal, it plays poorly post-flop against calls or 3-bets from the blinds. Folding to preserve chips and wait for a better spot is the most sound, low-variance decision early in the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 2h, an exceptionally weak hand. Since the Button has folded, it's just me and the Big Blind. While I could attempt a bluff raise, 42o is too weak to even complete the small blind with, as I would be playing out of position with a range disadvantage. The best play is to fold and move to the next hand.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit. This is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, even with positional advantage. In a three-handed game, while ranges are wider, J5o is still at the bottom of the distribution and not worth a speculative open or a call. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand or a better situation to utilize my stack.

Holding 9-5 offsuit in the small blind is a weak hand with very poor playability. Playing out of position against the Big Blind with such a disconnected hand is generally unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the disciplined choice here to conserve chips for better opportunities.

With a suited connector like 6d-7d on the button, I have a solid hand to open the action. Being in position allows me to play post-flop with an advantage. Opening with a raise to 300 is a standard strategic move to build the pot, take control of the initiative, and potentially steal the blinds given the passive play observed from my opponents.

Facing a 3bb button open from Flashlight, I have pocket fours in the small blind. The effective stacks are nearly 100bb deep, giving me excellent implied odds to set-mine. While playing out of position post-flop is a disadvantage, the deep stacks make calling to try and hit a set very profitable. I will flat call and re-evaluate on the flop, looking to continue only if I hit my set or have a highly favorable board.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 5s. Flashlight raised to 3x from the Button, and Pro called from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 900 chips. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds, which is more than enough to justify calling with a suited gapper that has good playability in multiway pots. Folding would be too tight given the price, and 3-betting into two players is unnecessary when I can simply close the action and see a flop.

I missed my set on a paired board (3h Qs Qd) multi-way. As the small blind out of position against the big blind and the pre-flop raiser, checking is the standard play. I will check and evaluate the action behind me, likely folding to any significant aggression.

The flop is 3h Qs Qd, which is a very dry, paired board. I hold 7s 5s, which has missed completely. In a three-way pot, especially when the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) is still to act, there is no merit in leading out with a bet. I have zero showdown value and very little equity. My plan is to check and likely fold to any significant aggression from Flashlight, as this board hits a button-opening range quite well (containing many Queens and pocket pairs).

The flop of 3h-Qs-Qd is very uncoordinated for my 6d-7d holdings. With two opponents checking, I am essentially holding air. Given that any Queen or pocket pair in their range has me dominated, a continuation bet into two players is high-risk and unlikely to force folds. Checking behind is the most prudent strategy to maintain pot control and see a free turn card while minimizing my exposure.

With pocket fours on a Q-Q-3-7 board multi-way, my hand has some marginal showdown value against missed overcards, but is very vulnerable to better pairs or a slow-played queen. The flop checked around, which usually limits the strength of my opponents' hands, but I am still out of position against two players. Betting here risks getting called or raised by better hands while only folding out worse ones. Checking allows me to control the pot size, and I will be prepared to fold if there is any significant aggression.

On a board of 3h Qs Qd 7c, I have turned a pair of Sevens. Pre-flop, Flashlight (the aggressor) raised and then checked the flop, which often indicates they do not have a Queen. Pro checked the flop and has now checked the turn. My pair of Sevens is likely the best hand currently, but it is not strong enough to bet for value against two opponents on such a dry board, as I would mostly be called by hands that beat me (like pocket pairs 88-JJ or a slow-played Queen). Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or catch a bluff from Flashlight.

With a middle pair of 7s on a board paired with Queens, my hand has some showdown value but is essentially a bluff catcher. Both opponents have checked twice, suggesting they are either weak or trapping. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and risks inflating the pot when I am likely behind any Queen. Checking behind is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown.

The river gave me a full house (Fours full of Queens)! Since the flop and turn checked around, it's unlikely either opponent has a Queen, but I have the effective nuts regardless. I need to extract value. A bet of half the pot is a good sizing to get called by middle pocket pairs, a 7, or even an Ace-high that suspects I am bluffing at a pot that was checked down.

I am in a three-way pot with a pair of Sevens (7s 5s) on a board of 3h-Qs-Qd-7c-4c. The pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) checked both the flop and the turn, which likely removes a Queen or a high pocket pair from their range. Pro checked the flop and turn as well, but has now led out for 450 (half the pot) on the river. While a pair of Sevens is a decent hand on such a dry board, Pro is betting into two opponents. This 'donk' lead on the river often signifies a hand that was slow-played or a thin value bet with a medium pocket pair (88-JJ) that now feels safe. Given that I only have third pair and Flashlight is still to act behind me, calling is risky. I do not have enough evidence that Pro is bluffing frequently enough to justify a call with 7-high kicker. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Pro's river bet of 450 after checking the flop and turn is a polarized line. They are representing a strong hand like a Queen, but the board is dry and the check-check-check line on the earlier streets suggests they may be turning a marginal hand into a bluff or betting a weak value hand like a smaller pair. My hand, two pair (Sevens and Queens), is a very strong bluff-catcher here, and with pot odds of 3:1 (offering 1350 to win by calling 450), calling is a mathematically sound decision to test Pro's range.

Pocket Kings (Kd Ks) is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Being first to act on the Button, a standard 3x raise is ideal to build the pot, take control of the hand, and extract value from worse hands that the blinds might defend with.

I am holding 6s 5s in the small blind. This is a solid suited connector that plays well in deep-stacked situations due to its high implied odds. Pro opened for 300 from the button, which is a standard late-position raise. I need to call 250 more to see the flop. With the current pot at 450, I'm getting roughly 2.8:1 odds. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the hand's potential to flop strong draws or disguised two-pairs makes it worth a call to see a flop. Folding is too tight for 3-handed play, and 3-betting is an option but calling keeps the pot manageable with a speculative hand.

I am in the big blind facing a raise to 300 from the button and a call from the small blind. The pot currently contains 700 chips. I need to call 200 more to see the flop, which offers excellent pot odds (200 to win 900, roughly 22%). My hand, 8c-Tc, is well-suited for multi-way pots, has decent connectivity, and represents a standard defense in this spot given the attractive price.

I have flopped very little equity on this Ace-high board, with only a backdoor spade flush draw. Given that I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and there is another player (Flashlight) behind me, checking is the standard and most prudent play. I will see how the other players react to this board, which heavily favors the button's raising range.

The flop of Jc-4s-As is extremely dry and heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (the button). My hand, 8c-Tc, has failed to connect with the board, offering only a very weak backdoor flush draw. Betting here would be a pure bluff into two opponents on a board texture that likely hit the aggressor's range hard, which is a poor strategic choice. Checking is the standard and correct play to see how the betting action develops.

With pocket Kings on an Ace-high flop in a 3-way pot, our hand is vulnerable. The blinds' pre-flop calling ranges contain many Ax combinations. Since both opponents checked, checking behind is the best option to control the pot size, avoid a check-raise from an Ace or strong draw, and see a free turn card. We also hold the King of Spades, giving us some backdoor equity and a blocker, but not enough to justify betting for value or protection right now.

I have 6-high on a board with three broadway cards (A, Q, J). After everyone checked the flop, the Queen of Diamonds on the turn makes the board even more dangerous for my range. I have no real equity or draws (only a backdoor spade flush draw). Checking is the only sensible play here as a bluff is unlikely to succeed against two opponents on this texture. I will check and likely fold to any bet.

The board texture is Ace-high and quite connected (A-Q-J-4), which is likely to have hit the ranges of my opponents. I have an 8-high hand with a gutshot straight draw (needing a King to complete). Since the flop was checked around, it is unlikely either player has a strong hand, but betting into two players on this board with only a gutshot draw is high-variance and inefficient. Checking is the standard play to preserve my stack, keep the pot size manageable, and potentially realize my equity for free if the river completes my straight.

With pocket Kings on a board of A-Q-J-4, our hand is essentially a bluff-catcher or weak showdown hand. There are many higher pairs (Aces and Queens) and two-pair combinations in the opponents' ranges. Even though both opponents checked again, betting here will mostly get called by better hands (like an Ace or a Queen) and fold out worse hands. Checking controls the pot size and takes us to the river for free, where we can evaluate our showdown value or hit our gutshot straight draw.

The board has become extremely coordinated with Ace, Queen, Jack, and Ten. Any King now makes a straight (A-K-Q-J-T). I have 6-high, which has no showdown value. Given that there are two other players in the hand and the board texture is so heavy with broadway cards, a bluff is unlikely to succeed. Everyone has checked through the flop and turn, which might indicate weakness, but it could also mean someone is trapping or playing a marginal hand cautiously. I will check and fold to any bet, as I cannot win this pot without a successful multi-way bluff on a very dangerous board.

The board is A-Q-J-10-4, which is extremely dangerous. Anyone holding a King has a straight (A-K-Q-J-10), and a large portion of the opponent's continuing range includes Aces, Queens, or Jacks, all of which beat my pair of Tens. Since the flop and turn were checked around, it's possible no one has a strong hand, but betting into two opponents on a board this coordinated with only a pair of Tens is effectively a bluff with very low equity against calling ranges. Checking behind to see the showdown is the only logical play.

With the Ten on the river, the board is A-Q-J-T-4. Our pocket Kings give us the Ace-high nut straight (Broadway). There are no possible flushes or full houses on this board, so we hold the absolute, invincible nuts. After both opponents checked to us again, we must bet to extract value. A half-pot bet of 450 is a reasonable sizing to get called by hands like top pair (an Ace), two pair, or a weaker straight if an opponent happens to hold a 9 or K.

I have 6-high on a board of As-Jc-4s-Qd-Td. After checking through the flop and turn, Pro (the pre-flop aggressor) has bet 450 into a 900 pot on the river. This board is extremely coordinated, and any King makes a straight. While Pro's checks on the flop and turn could indicate weakness, they could also be trapping or playing a marginal hand. Regardless of Pro's range, I have zero showdown value with 6-high and cannot win the pot by calling. A bluff-raise is extremely risky given the board texture and the fact that Flashlight is still in the hand. Therefore, folding is the only logical and disciplined action.

The board is A-Q-J-10-4. This is a very coordinated board where any King makes a broadway straight. Even without a King, any Ace, Queen, or Jack makes a better pair than my pair of Tens. Pro betting into two players after checking the flop and turn suggests they either have the straight or are polarized. With such a dangerous board and minimal showdown value, calling is a losing play as I am almost certainly beaten.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a weak starting hand that doesn't play well post-flop. While I could attempt a steal, the Big Blind is currently the chip leader and has been active in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary risk and wait for a stronger opening hand.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. My hand, Seven-Ten suited, is a respectable holding in an SB vs BB scenario. Calling the additional 50 chips to see a flop is a standard, low-variance play to exploit my position for the rest of the hand. I prefer this over raising, which might bloat the pot with a marginal hand against a tough opponent like Pro.

I have 3s 5c in the Big Blind after the Small Blind limped. This is a very weak hand with low value, but since I am in the Big Blind facing a limp, I can check and see a free flop. There is no need to bloat the pot or attempt a bluff pre-flop with this holding.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a highly connected, wet board (6-5-7). While checking is a viable option to control the pot, betting allows me to protect my hand against various straight draws and flush draws that could easily outdraw me on the turn. A small bet of 100 chips is sufficient to test Pro's reaction and gather information about the strength of their hand.

On a highly coordinated 6c 5d 7c flop, I have bottom pair and a gutshot straight draw. However, my gutshot is to the 'idiot end' of the straight (a 4 would give any player with an 8 a higher straight). Given the weak showdown value of bottom pair and the heavily tainted draw, continuing against a bet is unprofitable. The best play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Q6o is a weak hand with low equity. Given the current tournament stage and the goal to maximize stack size, playing marginal hands out of position or in the button is not optimal. Folding preserves my stack for better starting hands.
